content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 22, 2017

James R. Williams
Office of the County Counsel
County of Santa Clara
70 West Hedding Street
East Wing, 9th Floor
San Jose, CA 95110

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-060

Dear Mr. Williams:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

May Supervisor Chavez participate in the County's decision-making process relating to the potential upcoming sale of approximately six acres of County property to the Housing Authority of County of Santa Clara ("Housing Authority") considering that she owns a residence located approximately 900 feet from the property to be sold?

CONCLUSION

Yes. Supervisor Chavez may participate in the decision-making process relating to the potential upcoming sale of County property to the Housing Authority because there will be no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her real property interest.

_______________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-060
Page No. 2

FACTS

The Santa Clara County Board of Supervisors will consider the sale of 6.1 acres of vacant land² to the Housing Authority generally located along and between 13ᵗʰ and 17ᵗʰ Streets, and along and between East St. John Street and East Santa Clara Streets in the City of San José (the "Property"). The San Jose Medical Center had previously been located on the property, but the structures were raised after the County acquired the Property in 2010. If the Housing Authority acquires the Property, its intent is to land-bank the Property, with the ultimate future goal to use the property for affordable housing and other mixed-uses.

Supervisor Chavez's residence is located approximately 900 feet from the nearest property line of the Property, and separated from the Property by several streets, many homes, commercial properties, and medical offices.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest rules prohibit a public official from making, participating in making, or using his or her official position in any way to influence a governmental decision in which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a "financial interest." (Section 87100.) A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated interests. (Section 87103.)

Financial Interest

The interest here is residential real property in which Supervisor Chavez has an interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

Foreseeability and Materiality

For a financial interest not "explicitly involved" in the decision at issue, as here, the financial effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) Regulation 18702.2 provides the relevant materiality standards for financial interests in real property. None of the criteria provided in this regulation are met under the facts you have provided.

Additionally, Regulation 18702 provides that "the financial effect of a governmental interests decision is not material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant." The County's decision to sell the real property to the Housing Authority would have only a nominal, inconsequential or insignificant effect upon the Supervisor's real property that is located approximately nine hundred feet away and separated from the Property by several streets, many homes, commercial properties, and medical offices. Any potential financial effect upon Supervisor Chavez's real property would be

² In a follow up telephone conversation, your office noted that the terms of the sale had been revised since the mailing of the request for advice; to include 6.1 acres of property, with no structures.

File No. A-17-060
Page No. 3

occur only after future governmental decisions are made about what is to be built on the property.
Therefore, there will be no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon her financial
interest as a result of the current decision before the Board of Supervisors.

Since there is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Supervisor Chavez's
financial interest, she does not have a conflict of interest in the current decision. However, please
keep in mind that future governmental decisions regarding the Property, such as those involving
what is to be constructed on the property, could have a financial effect upon her property.

Accordingly, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit Supervisor Chavez's from taking
part in governmental decisions relating to the proposed sale of approximately six acres of County
property to the Housing Authority of County of Santa Clara.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
